subtitle: Journal-club style ICU update: PICO, key results, appraisal, bedside impact, and medical English.

script.heading: Intro
sentences: Welcome to Critical Care Daily for June 29, 2026.
Today we will review 3 recent ICU-relevant publications.
The format is quick listen first, then PICO, key results, critical appraisal, bedside impact, and medical English notes.
Use this as a morning triage tool, then open the source link for full-text appraisal before changing practice.

script.heading: Story 1 - Balanced crystalloids versus saline and mortality in hospitali
sentences: Story 1 is about Balanced crystalloids versus saline and mortality in hospitalized patients: a hierarchical Bayesian meta-analysis of cluster-randomized trials..
This is a systematic review / meta-analysis from Journal of critical care.

The clinical question is best framed as follows: in Critically ill or ICU-relevant patients described in the abstract; confirm eligibility criteria in the full text., how should we interpret Balanced crystalloids compared with saline and mortality in hospitalized patients: a hierarchical Bayesian meta-analysis of cluster-randomized trials?
Six trials involving 42,223 patients met the inclusion criteria.
For mortality, the pooled Bayesian relative effect was 0.92 (95% CrI 0.77-1.18), corresponding to an 83% posterior probability of benefit.
For AKI, the pooled Bayesian relative effect was 1.02 (95% CrI 0.86-1.23), corresponding to a 40% posterior probability of benefit.
For critical appraisal, focus on whether the design supports a causal conclusion and whether the patients resemble your ICU population.

The bedside implication is this: This is relevant to default ICU fluid choice; focus on absolute mortality difference, kidney outcomes, and whether the enrolled population matches your resuscitation patients.
Useful English phrase: these findings should be interpreted cautiously because the details of design and applicability matter.

script.heading: Story 2 - Comparison of Intensive Versus Conventional Glycemic Control T
sentences: Story 2 is about Comparison of Intensive Versus Conventional Glycemic Control Targets: An Updated Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis of the 2024 Society of Critical Care Medicine Guidelines on Glycemic Control for Critically Ill Adults..
This is a systematic review / meta-analysis from Critical care medicine.

The clinical question is best framed as follows: in critically ill adults on insulin infusions, how should we interpret Comparison of Intensive compared with Conventional Glycemic Control Targets: An Updated Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis of the 2024 Society of Critical Care Medicine Guidelines on Glycemic Control for Critically Ill Adults?
Forty-five RCTs were included involving 32,215 patients.
No differences were seen between INT and CONV targets for hospital mortality or ICU mortality.
Most of the studies with significant differences contained serious inconsistencies or risk of bias.
For critical appraisal, focus on whether the design supports a causal conclusion and whether the patients resemble your ICU population.
The bedside implication is this: For most ICU patients, this supports avoiding very tight glucose targets unless a protocol can reliably prevent severe hypoglycemia.
Useful English phrase: these findings should be interpreted cautiously because the details of design and applicability matter.

script.heading: Story 3 - Efficacy and Safety of Systemic Prophylactic Antibacterials in
sentences: Story 3 is about Efficacy and Safety of Systemic Prophylactic Antibacterials in Mechanically Ventilated Patients: Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis..
This is a systematic review / meta-analysis from Critical care medicine.

The clinical question is best framed as follows: in patients, how should we interpret Exposure or intervention stated in the title/objective. compared with Comparator or control group stated in the methods/results, if present.?
Eleven RCTs with 5562 patients were included.

Meta-analysis results showed that the systemic prophylactic use of antibacterials probably reduces the incidence of VAP (relative risk [RR], 0.65; 95% CI, 0.55-0.77; moderate certainty) and the incidence of early VAP (RR, 0.52; 95% CI, 0.41-0.66; moderate certainty), but probably does not reduce in-hospital mortality (RR, 0.85; 95% CI, 0.68-1.06; moderate certainty) or duration of mechanical ventilation (mean difference [MD], -0.32; 95% CI, -0.79 to 0.16; moderate certainty), and may not reduce length of hospital stay (MD, -1.56; 95% CI, -5.15 to 2.02; low certainty).
Previous studies have shown that selective decontamination of the digestive tract can reduce the incidence and mortality of ventilator-associated pneumonia (VAP) in mechanically ventilated patients.
For critical appraisal, focus on whether the design supports a causal conclusion and whether the patients resemble your ICU population.

The bedside implication is this: This may affect infection-prevention policy for ventilated patients, but adoption should depend on local resistance ecology, stewardship review, and patient-centered outcome size.
Useful English phrase: these findings should be interpreted cautiously because the details of design and applicability matter.

script.heading: Outro
sentences: That wraps up today's journal club style update.
Before applying any finding, check the full source, the absolute effect size, and your local ICU context.
For English practice, repeat one academic phrase from each article out loud.

articles.title: Balanced crystalloids versus saline and mortality in hospitalized patients: a hierarchical Bayesian meta-analysis of cluster-randomized trials.
articles.summary: Balanced crystalloids are widely used as alternatives to 0.9% saline, yet their effects on patient-important outcomes remain uncertain. Six trials involving 42,223 patients met the inclusion criteria. For mortality, the pooled Bayesian relative effect was 0.92 (95% CrI 0.77-1.18), corresponding to an 83% posterior probability of benefit. For AKI, the pooled Bayesian relative effect was 1.02 (95% CrI 0.86-1.23), corresponding to a 40% posterior probability of benefit. Balanced crystalloid policies were not associated with a clear reduction in mortality or AKI in cluster-randomized evaluations of routine care.
articles.analysis: This is relevant to default ICU fluid choice; focus on absolute mortality difference, kidney outcomes, and whether the enrolled population matches your resuscitation patients.

pico.comparator: saline and mortality in hospitalized patients: a hierarchical Bayesian meta-analysis of cluster-randomized trials
pico.outcomes: Six trials involving 42,223 patients met the inclusion criteria. For mortality, the pooled Bayesian relative effect was 0.92 (95% CrI 0.77-1.18), corresponding to an 83% posterior probability of benefit. For AKI, the pooled Bayesian relative effect was 1.02 (95% CrI 0.86-1.23), corresponding to a 40% posterior probability of benefit. Sensitivity analyses...

keyResults: Six trials involving 42,223 patients met the inclusion criteria.
For mortality, the pooled Bayesian relative effect was 0.92 (95% CrI 0.77-1.18), corresponding to an 83% posterior probability of benefit.
For AKI, the pooled Bayesian relative effect was 1.02 (95% CrI 0.86-1.23), corresponding to a 40% posterior probability of benefit.
criticalAppraisal: High-yield synthesis: check heterogeneity, included study quality, and whether ICU patients match your setting.
Look for heterogeneity, small-study effects, and whether pooled outcomes are clinically comparable.
articles.practiceImpact: This is relevant to default ICU fluid choice; focus on absolute mortality difference, kidney outcomes, and whether the enrolled population matches your resuscitation patients.

articles.title: Comparison of Intensive Versus Conventional Glycemic Control Targets: An Updated Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis of the 2024 Society of Critical Care Medicine Guidelines on Glycemic Control for Critically Ill Adults.
articles.summary: Forty-five RCTs were included involving 32,215 patients. No differences were seen between INT and CONV targets for hospital mortality or ICU mortality. Most of the studies with significant differences contained serious inconsistencies or risk of bias. No differences were seen in ICU or hospital mortality. To perform an updated systematic review and meta-analysis of the efficacy and safety of intensive (INT) vs. conventional (CONV) blood glucose (BG) targets for critically ill adults on insulin infusions.
articles.analysis: For most ICU patients, this supports avoiding very tight glucose targets unless a protocol can reliably prevent severe hypoglycemia.

keyResults: Forty-five RCTs were included involving 32,215 patients.
No differences were seen between INT and CONV targets for hospital mortality or ICU mortality.
Most of the studies with significant differences contained serious inconsistencies or risk of bias.
No differences were seen in ICU or hospital mortality.

Look for heterogeneity, small-study effects, and whether pooled outcomes are clinically comparable.

articles.title: Efficacy and Safety of Systemic Prophylactic Antibacterials in Mechanically Ventilated Patients: Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis.
articles.summary: Eleven RCTs with 5562 patients were included. Meta-analysis results showed that the systemic prophylactic use of antibacterials probably reduces the incidence of VAP (relative risk [RR], 0.65; 95% CI, 0.55-0.77; moderate certainty) and the incidence of early VAP (RR, 0.52; 95% CI, 0.41-0.66; moderate certainty), but probably does not reduce in-hospital mortality (RR, 0.85; 95% CI, 0.68-1.06; moderate certainty) or duration of mechanical ventilation (mean difference [MD], -0.32; 95% CI, -0.79 to 0.16; moderate certainty), and may not reduce length of hospital stay (MD, -1.56; 95% CI, -5.15 to 2.02; low certainty). Previous studies have shown that selective decontamination of the digestive tract can reduce the incidence and mortality of ventilator-associated pneumonia (VAP) in mechanically ventilated patients. However, the prophylactic role of IV antibacterials, one of its essential components, remains unclear. Therefore, this study aims to evaluate the efficacy and safety of systemic prophylactic use of antibacterials in mechanically ventilated patients.
articles.analysis: This may affect infection-prevention policy for ventilated patients, but adoption should depend on local resistance ecology, stewardship review, and patient-centered outcome size.

pico.intervention: Exposure or intervention stated in the title/objective.
pico.comparator: Comparator or control group stated in the methods/results, if present.
pico.outcomes: Main outcomes are those reported in the abstract; prioritize patient-centered outcomes such as mortality, ventilation duration, ICU stay, and adverse events.

keyResults: Eleven RCTs with 5562 patients were included.

Meta-analysis results showed that the systemic prophylactic use of antibacterials probably reduces the incidence of VAP (relative risk [RR], 0.65; 95% CI, 0.55-0.77; moderate certainty) and the incidence of early VAP (RR, 0.52; 95% CI, 0.41-0.66; moderate certainty), but probably does not reduce in-hospital mortality (RR, 0.85; 95% CI, 0.68-1.06; moderate certainty) or duration of mechanical ventilation (mean difference [MD], -0.32; 95% CI, -0.79 to 0.16; moderate certainty), and may not reduce length of hospital stay (MD, -1.56; 95% CI, -5.15 to 2.02; low certainty).
Previous studies have shown that selective decontamination of the digestive tract can reduce the incidence and mortality of ventilator-associated pneumonia (VAP) in mechanically ventilated patients.
However, the prophylactic role of IV antibacterials, one of its essential components, remains unclear.
criticalAppraisal: High-yield synthesis: check heterogeneity, included study quality, and whether ICU patients match your setting.
Look for heterogeneity, small-study effects, and whether pooled outcomes are clinically comparable.
articles.practiceImpact: This may affect infection-prevention policy for ventilated patients, but adoption should depend on local resistance ecology, stewardship review, and patient-centered outcome size.
